title: What is a goroutine
instruction:
A goroutine is a function that runs concurrently with other functions.

Start one with the `go` keyword:

```
go someFunction()
```

That is it. One word. Go handles all the scheduling — no threads, no callbacks, no async/await.

Run this. Two functions run concurrently. Notice the output order might vary — both are running at the same time.
successMessage: Numbers and letters interleaved — two functions running at the same time. The go keyword is all it takes. In most languages this would require threads, thread pools, and a lot of boilerplate.

title: Channels — communicate between goroutines
instruction:
A channel is a pipe between goroutines. One goroutine sends a value, another receives it.

```
ch := make(chan int)   // create a channel
ch <- 42              // send 42 into the channel
value := <-ch         // receive from the channel
```

Sending and receiving block until both sides are ready — a built-in synchronisation mechanism.

Run this. The goroutine sends a result back through the channel. Main receives and prints it.
successMessage: The goroutine computed 7² and sent it back through the channel. Main blocked on <-ch until the result arrived. No shared variables, no locks — just a channel.

title: Send and receive multiple values
instruction:
A channel can send as many values as you need. The receiver collects them in order.

Start three goroutines, each computing a square and sending the result back. Receive all three results and print them.

Add a third goroutine that sends `square(9, ch)` and receive its result.
hint: go square(9, ch)  then result3 := <-ch  fmt.Println("9 squared:", result3)

codeChecks.message: Receive the third result from the channel.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Three goroutines, three results, one channel. The results may come back in any order — goroutines run concurrently and the channel collects them as they finish.

title: WaitGroup — wait for goroutines to finish
instruction:
`sync.WaitGroup` lets you wait for a group of goroutines to complete.

```
var wg sync.WaitGroup
wg.Add(1)        // one goroutine to wait for
go func() {
    defer wg.Done()  // signal when done
    // ...
}()
wg.Wait()        // block until all Done() are called
```

Run this. Three goroutines each process a job. `wg.Wait()` blocks until all three call `wg.Done()`.
successMessage: wg.Wait() blocked until all three goroutines called wg.Done(). The "All jobs complete" line printed last — guaranteed. WaitGroup is the standard way to coordinate a group of goroutines.

title: Buffered channels
instruction:
A regular channel blocks until the receiver is ready. A buffered channel has a queue — you can send without blocking until the buffer is full.

`ch := make(chan int, 3)` — buffer of 3. Send up to 3 values before anyone receives.

Run this. Values are sent without a matching receiver because the buffer holds them. Then they are drained one by one.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	ch := make(chan int, 3)

	ch <- 10
	ch <- 20
	ch <- 30

	fmt.Println(<-ch)
	fmt.Println(<-ch)
	fmt.Println(<-ch)
}
successMessage: No goroutines needed — the buffer held all three values. Buffered channels decouple senders from receivers. Use them when you know the maximum number of pending values ahead of time.

title: Range over a channel
instruction:
You can loop over a channel with `range` — it receives values until the channel is closed.

`close(ch)` signals that no more values will be sent. The range loop exits automatically.

Run this. The producer sends 5 values and closes the channel. The range loop receives all of them.
successMessage: range on a channel is clean and safe. The loop exits the moment the channel closes. Always close a channel from the sender side — closing a channel you are receiving from is a bug.

title: select — handle multiple channels
instruction:
`select` is like a `switch` for channels. It waits for whichever channel is ready first:

```
select {
case msg := <-ch1:
    fmt.Println("from ch1:", msg)
case msg := <-ch2:
    fmt.Println("from ch2:", msg)
}
```

If multiple channels are ready simultaneously, Go picks one at random.

Run this. Two goroutines send at different speeds. `select` handles whichever arrives first.
successMessage: Fast arrived first, slow second. select handles whichever channel is ready — no polling, no busy-waiting. This is how Go implements timeouts, cancellations, and fan-in patterns.

title: select with default — non-blocking
instruction:
Add a `default` case to `select` to make it non-blocking. If no channel is ready, the default runs immediately.

```
select {
case msg := <-ch:
    fmt.Println(msg)
default:
    fmt.Println("nothing ready yet")
}
```

Run this. The channel has nothing yet — the default fires. Then a value is sent and the channel case fires.
successMessage: Non-blocking channel reads — perfect for checking queues, implementing try-locks, or polling without sleeping. The default case turns select into a non-blocking peek.

title: Mutex — protect shared data
instruction:
When multiple goroutines access the same variable, you get a race condition — unpredictable results.

`sync.Mutex` protects shared data with a lock:

```
var mu sync.Mutex
mu.Lock()
sharedVar++
mu.Unlock()
```

Or use `defer mu.Unlock()` right after `Lock()` to always unlock even if the function panics.

Run this. A counter is incremented by 100 goroutines safely.
successMessage: 100 goroutines, 100 increments, exactly 100 every time. Without the mutex you would get random results between 1 and 100. The mutex ensures only one goroutine touches value at a time.

title: Done channel — signal cancellation
instruction:
A done channel signals goroutines to stop.

`close(done)` broadcasts to everyone waiting on it — all goroutines reading from a closed channel receive the zero value immediately.

Run this. The worker goroutine checks `<-done` via select. When main closes the channel, the worker exits cleanly.
successMessage: close(done) broadcast the stop signal to the worker instantly. The empty struct{} channel uses zero memory — chan struct{} is the idiomatic Go signal channel. context.Context builds on this same idea.

title: Worker pool
instruction:
A worker pool runs a fixed number of goroutines to process jobs from a queue channel.

This limits resource usage — instead of spawning 10,000 goroutines, you run 3 workers that each process many jobs.

Run this. 3 workers process 9 jobs from the jobs channel. Add 3 more jobs to the queue.
hint: for i := 10; i <= 12; i++ { jobs <- i }  (add before close(jobs))

codeChecks.message: Send job 10 to the jobs channel.
Send job 11 to the jobs channel.
Send job 12 to the jobs channel.
Replace the TODO comment with the three new jobs.

successMessage: 12 jobs, 3 workers, sum of 1²+2²+...+12² = 650. Worker pools are everywhere in Go — HTTP servers, background processors, batch pipelines. Fixed workers, unlimited jobs.

title: Race condition — spot and fix it
instruction:
A race condition happens when two goroutines access the same variable without synchronisation. The result is unpredictable.

The code below has a race condition on `count`. Run it a few times — the result varies.

Fix it by adding a `sync.Mutex` to protect `count`.
hint: Add var mu sync.Mutex above the loop. Inside the goroutine: mu.Lock(); count++; mu.Unlock() — or use defer.

codeChecks.message: Add a sync.Mutex to protect the count variable.

successMessage: Count: 50, every single time. The mutex serialises access to count — only one goroutine at a time. Run `go test -race` on your projects to catch race conditions automatically.

title: Goroutines are cheap
instruction:
A goroutine starts with about 2KB of stack memory (growing as needed). You can run thousands without issue.

This example launches 1,000 goroutines. Each increments a counter. In most languages this would be thousands of OS threads — each using megabytes. In Go it is lightweight and fast.

Run this.
successMessage: 1,000 goroutines, exact count, no locks needed (atomic operations). In real Go servers, tens of thousands of goroutines run concurrently — one per incoming request — without breaking a sweat.

title: Build a concurrent pipeline
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a three-stage pipeline:

1. `generate(nums ...int) <-chan int` — sends numbers into a channel, closes it when done
2. `square(in <-chan int) <-chan int` — receives numbers, sends their squares
3. `filter(in <-chan int, min int) <-chan int` — only passes through values ≥ min

In main: generate 1–10, square them, filter results ≥ 25, print each passing value.

Expected output (squares of 5,6,7,8,9,10 that are ≥ 25):
```
25
36
49
64
81
100
```
hint: Each stage: make a channel, start a goroutine that reads from in and writes to out, returns the out channel.

codeChecks.message: Define a generate function that returns a channel.
Define a square function that squares values from a channel.
Define a filter function that filters values from a channel.
Each stage should return a <-chan int (receive-only channel).
Close the output channel when your goroutine is done.
Use range to read from channels in each stage.

successMessage: A three-stage concurrent pipeline. Numbers flow through stages like water through pipes — generated, squared, filtered — each stage running concurrently. This is how Go handles data streaming at scale. You now know concurrency.
